repo.description: Vim-fork focused on extensibility and usability
repo.name: neovim

body:
This PR makes `vim.pack.get()` more capable when it comes to getting data about pending updates. In particular:

- Field `rev` is now taken directly from the Git repo if `opts.info=true` and from the lockfile if `opts.info=false` (instead of always from the lockfile). This makes `vim.pack.get()` slower (due to one extra `git ...` call per plugin), but `opts.info=true` is designed to be informative and not necessarily fast.
- Add field `rev_version` that points to the revision of the resolved `spec.version`. This can be used to determine if running `vim.pack.update({ 'plugin-name' }, { offline = true })` will result into updating plugin's state. I.e. if there are pending (local) updates, like after switching `spec.version` or skipping previous update(s).
- Add `opts.offline` (with default `true`, unlike in `vim.pack.update()`) to be able to control whether to fetch new updates from plugin source. This way `vim.pack.get({ 'plugin-name' }, { offline = false })` can be used to determine if running `vim.pack.update({ 'plugin-name' })` will result into updating plugin's state. I.e. if there are pending updates from the source.

---

Resolve #39285

---

I am only about 90% happy with the name `rev_version` for the Git revision of the resolved version. It might be confused with "revision version". Other candidates that I though about:
- `version_rev`- I think having the same prefix as `rev` is better.
- `rev_to` - a bit ambiguous.
- `rev_update` - the framing of "revision of resolved version" seems better than "revision that would be applied after `update()`".

Maybe I am overthinking this, though.

comment_data.body:
I don't get why `resolved spec.version` is mentioned here. The `rev` field is/was also resolved from a spec.version, right...?

```suggestion
        • {rev_version}? (`string`) Git revision of a pending update.
```
comment_data.diff_hunk:
@@ -496,7 +503,10 @@ get({names}, {opts})                                          *vim.pack.get()*
         • {branches}? (`string[]`) Available Git branches (first is default).
           Missing if `info=false`.
         • {path} (`string`) Plugin's path on disk.
-        • {rev} (`string`) Current Git revision.
+        • {rev} (`string`) Current Git revision. Taken from
+          |vim.pack-lockfile| if `info=false`.
+        • {rev_version}? (`string`) Git revision of a resolved `spec.version`.

comment_data.body:
> The `rev` field is/was also resolved from a spec.version, right...?

[Not quite](https://github.com/neovim/neovim/pull/39820#issuecomment-4470849345).
comment_data.diff_hunk:
@@ -496,7 +503,10 @@ get({names}, {opts})                                          *vim.pack.get()*
         • {branches}? (`string[]`) Available Git branches (first is default).
           Missing if `info=false`.
         • {path} (`string`) Plugin's path on disk.
-        • {rev} (`string`) Current Git revision.
+        • {rev} (`string`) Current Git revision. Taken from
+          |vim.pack-lockfile| if `info=false`.
+        • {rev_version}? (`string`) Git revision of a resolved `spec.version`.

comment_regular_data.body:
> I am only about 90% happy with the name `rev_version` for the Git revision of the resolved version. It might be confused with "revision version". Other candidates that I though about:
> 
> * `version_rev`- I think having the same prefix as `rev` is better.
> * `rev_to` - a bit ambiguous.
> * `rev_update` - the framing of "revision of resolved version" seems better than "revision that would be applied after `update()`".

how about `rev_new` ? else `rev_to` or something like that seems much more intuitive, to me. IIUC, it should convey that "this is the *incoming*/pending/upstream revision"?

`rev_version` had me puzzled

> IIUC, it should convey that "this is the _incoming_/pending/upstream revision"?

Maybe somewhat, but not 100%.

Assume these steps:
- `:lua vim.pack.add({ 'https://github.com/user/plugin' })`. It installs and checks out default branch.
- `:restart` and `:lua vim.pack.add({ { src = 'https://github.com/user/plugin', version = 'dev' } })`. Now plugin's `version` points to `'dev'`, but the state on disk is not yet updated to it. In this case `rev` is current, while `rev_version` points to the revision of `'dev'` branch.

This same logic applies to new updates from source. Fetching only changes where the plugin's `spec.version` (like `main` branch) points to.

By its nature, running `vim.pack.update()` wants to change the current revision to one where `spec.version` points at. It also serves as a way to fetch/download updates from source prior to that.

> how about `rev_new` ? else `rev_to` or something like that seems much more intuitive, to me. IIUC, it should convey that "this is the _incoming_/pending/upstream revision"?

I am not particularly fond of `rev_new`, as there is nothing "new" per se. The `rev_to` is a bit ambiguous, but may work if you didn't find the above explanation convincing enough.

Is this a correct understanding:

- `rev` is the "on disk" rev
- `rev_??` is the "pending" rev, resolved per `version` according to the latest fetch

anyway, mainly hoping we can make the difference clear and intuitive. `rev_version` makes some sense but it will be confusing for most users. 

it seems to me like the central point is "current on-disk" vs "pending/incoming". so choosing a name that conveys that would be ideal. I think `rev_version` requires too much familiarity with the details.

`rev_to` is fine imo. `rev_pending` ?

> Is this a correct understanding:
> 
>     * `rev` is the "on disk" rev
> 
>     * `rev_??` is the "pending" rev, resolved per `version` according to the latest fetch

Correct. Maybe with an asterisk of "resolved per *current* `version`" to indicate that it also works when `spec.version` has changed.

> it seems to me like the central point is "current on-disk" vs "pending/incoming". so choosing a name that conveys that would be ideal. I think `rev_version` requires too much familiarity with the details.
> 
> `rev_to` is fine imo. `rev_pending` ?

That was my concern indeed. `rev_to` it is then.

> Add `opts.offline` (with default `true`, unlike in `vim.pack.update()`) to be able to control whether to fetch new updates from plugin source.

Doesn't this block while the web request is resolved? It makes me think we might not want to add this functionality to `vim.pack.get()`, and instead have a new function which either accepts a callback function or returns a Future/Promise-type object.
the purpose of `info` was to allow opt-in to more costly stuff. though I don't think this PR closes the door on an optional `on_info` callback, that is something we can explore.

> Doesn't this block while the web request is resolved? It makes me think we might not want to add this functionality to `vim.pack.get()`, and instead have a new function which either accepts a callback function or returns a Future/Promise-type object.

I am not really fond of the idea of the new function just for "async get". The only reasonable use case for this is "check for pending updates on startup" which I think is not a good workflow to cultivate. Checking for updates should be a deliberate action.

> though I don't think this PR closes the door on an optional `on_info` callback, that is something we can explore.

Yeah, potentially it is possible. But I'd be careful about adding it as this will make the code more spaghetti-like while not serving a lot of new use cases.

> The only reasonable use case for this is "check for pending updates on startup" which I think is not a good workflow to cultivate. Checking for updates should be a deliberate action.

If I choose to configure Neovim to check for updates on startup, that _is_ a deliberate action. As for what constitutes a good workflow: That's subjective/situational, and therefore should also be left for the end user to decide, IMO.

Recommendations and reasonable defaults are helpful and appreciated, but "good" is not a universal concept. Microsoft, Google and Apple have problems letting users decide what's good for them; it would be a shame to fall into that trap.

title: Make `vim.pack.get()` return data about updates
